mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment, handling lifecycle tasks such as deployment, upgrades, and backups. It is ideal for users looking to simplify MySQL cluster management while ensuring high availability and scalability. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the MySQL Operator Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install the operator using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the MySQL Operator in a dedicated namespace, creating the namespace if it does not already exist.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace --version 9.0.0

```

> This installs the MySQL Operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Operator Installation
steps.description:
Check that the MySQL Operator deployment is running correctly in the specified namespace.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> This command verifies the status of the MySQL Operator deployment.

steps.title: Create MySQL Root User Secret
steps.description:
Create a Kubernetes secret that contains the credentials for the MySQL root user, which is necessary for managing the InnoDB Cluster.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> This command creates a secret named 'mypwds' with the root user credentials.

steps.title: Deploy MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
Define and deploy a MySQL InnoDB Cluster using a custom resource that references the previously created secret.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.30'
EOF

```

> This command creates an InnoDB Cluster named 'mycluster'.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.30'
EOF

steps.title: Verify InnoDB Cluster Deployment
steps.description:
Check the status of the InnoDB Cluster to ensure it has been created successfully.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator

```

> This command lists all InnoDB Clusters in the mysql-operator namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if persistent volumes are not handled properly.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> This command uninstalls the MySQL Operator from the specified namespace.
> This command deletes the namespace, which may leave orphaned resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your data. This process will upgrade the MySQL Operator to a new version.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator -o yaml > backup-innodbclusters.yaml

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --version 9.0.1

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Backup existing InnoDB Cluster configurations.
> This command upgrades the MySQL Operator to the specified version.
> Verify that the MySQL Operator deployment is running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator -o yaml > backup-innodbclusters.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create InnoDB Cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The secret containing MySQL root user credentials may be missing or incorrect.

**Fix:**
Verify the secret exists and contains the correct credentials using 'kubectl get secret mypwds -o yaml'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'MySQL Operator not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL Operator deployment may not be running or may have failed to install.

**Fix:**
Check the deployment status with 'kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The InnoDB Cluster may be in the process of initializing or there may be insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the InnoDB Cluster with 'kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient permissions'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL Operator may not have the necessary RBAC permissions.

**Fix:**
Ensure the ClusterRole and RoleBindings are correctly configured for the MySQL Operator.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.0.30, improved backup and restore capabilities.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for MySQL versions below 8.0.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from version 8.4, ensure to update your InnoDB Cluster configurations to match the new version requirements.

resolution.summary: You should see the InnoDB Cluster listed with 'kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator' showing the status as 'Ready'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.30'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources and tools installed.
